phase1.explanation:
Sets are collections with no duplicates:

HashSet:
• No order guaranteed
• O(1) average for add/contains/remove
• Based on hashCode() for performance
• Allows one null value

TreeSet:
• Sorted in natural order
• O(log n) for add/contains/remove
• Implements SortedSet
• No null values (NullPointerException)

Common operations:
• add(element) — adds if not already present
• contains(element) — checks membership
• remove(element) — removes if present
• size() — number of elements
• clear() — removes all

TreeSet-specific:
• first() — smallest element
• last() — largest element
• lower(element) — greatest element < given
• higher(element) — smallest element > given

When to use HashSet:
• Need fast membership testing
• Don't need order
• Removing duplicates

When to use TreeSet:
• Need sorted order
• Need range operations (between x and y)

⚠️ Important: For custom objects in HashSet, you MUST override equals() and hashCode()!

phase2.errorMessage:
Size: 2

(No error — but the LOGIC is WRONG! Duplicates appear because equals/hashCode are missing.)

phase3.prompt:
Write a program that:
1. Creates an ArrayList<Integer> with numbers: [1, 2, 3, 2, 4, 3, 5, 1, 6]
2. Creates a HashSet<Integer> from the list (removing duplicates)
3. Prints the HashSet
4. Creates a TreeSet<Integer> from the same list
5. Prints the TreeSet (should be sorted)
6. Prints the size of both sets
7. Checks if both sets contain the number 4
